message.content:
Adversaries may exploit startup items in macOS to establish persistence by creating directories and files in the /Library/StartupItems directory. These items, which include shell scripts or executables and configuration files, execute during the final phase of the boot process, running with elevated root privileges. Although this method is deprecated and not guaranteed to exist on all systems, it remains present in macOS Sierra. Tools like jRAT can manage and list these startup entries, making it easier for adversaries to maintain control over compromised systems. 

The potential impact includes unauthorized persistent access, privilege escalation, and the ability to execute malicious code with root privileges. Common exploitation methods involve creating or modifying startup items to include malicious scripts or executables.

Mitigation strategies include regularly auditing the /Library/StartupItems directory for unauthorized entries, employing endpoint detection and response (EDR) solutions to monitor changes to startup items, and ensuring systems are updated to use more secure initialization methods like Launch Daemons. Additionally, restricting write permissions to critical system directories can help prevent unauthorized modifications.
